texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Are Large Traders Harmed by Front-running HFTs?
texts.text: Are Large Traders Harmed by Front-running HFTs?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ziyi Xu a , Xue Cheng a,1
texts.text: Ziyi Xu a , Xue Cheng a,1

texts.label: text
texts.orig: a LMEQF, Department of Financial Mathematics, School of Mathematical Sciences, Peking University, Beijing 100871, China.
texts.text: a LMEQF, Department of Financial Mathematics, School of Mathematical Sciences, Peking University, Beijing 100871, China.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper studies the influences of a high-frequency trader (HFT) on a large trader whose future trading is predicted by the former. We conclude that HFT always front-runs and the large trader is benefited when: (1) there is sufficient high-speed noise trading; (2) HFT's prediction is vague enough. Besides, we find surprisingly that (1) making HFT's prediction less accurate might decrease large trader's profit; (2) when there is little high-speed noise trading, although HFT nearly does nothing, the large trader is still hurt.
texts.text: This paper studies the influences of a high-frequency trader (HFT) on a large trader whose future trading is predicted by the former. We conclude that HFT always front-runs and the large trader is benefited when: (1) there is sufficient high-speed noise trading; (2) HFT's prediction is vague enough. Besides, we find surprisingly that (1) making HFT's prediction less accurate might decrease large trader's profit; (2) when there is little high-speed noise trading, although HFT nearly does nothing, the large trader is still hurt.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Front-runner predicts large traders' incoming orders, trades in the same direction ahead of them, and then closes the positions when they arrive, hoping to profit from the price impacts caused by large traders. For example, if a front-runner asserts that an investor is going to buy, she will buy in advance and then sell back to the investor.
texts.text: Front-runner predicts large traders' incoming orders, trades in the same direction ahead of them, and then closes the positions when they arrive, hoping to profit from the price impacts caused by large traders. For example, if a front-runner asserts that an investor is going to buy, she will buy in advance and then sell back to the investor.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As found in Kirilenko et al. (2017) [1], in today's market, most front-runners are high-frequency traders (HFTs), who process information faster and send orders with lower latency. The development of high-frequency trading makes front-running a major concern.
texts.text: As found in Kirilenko et al. (2017) [1], in today's market, most front-runners are high-frequency traders (HFTs), who process information faster and send orders with lower latency. The development of high-frequency trading makes front-running a major concern.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Conventional wisdom has it that front-runners increase the large trader's costs by taking away cheaper liquidity. However, some studies do believe that under certain conditions, front-runners could be beneficial. Empirical work supports both views, a unified conclusion has not been reached.
texts.text: Conventional wisdom has it that front-runners increase the large trader's costs by taking away cheaper liquidity. However, some studies do believe that under certain conditions, front-runners could be beneficial. Empirical work supports both views, a unified conclusion has not been reached.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, through an extended celebrated Kyle's [2] model, we focus on the influences of an HFT on an informed large trader whose future order is detected by the former. We prove that it is optimal for HFT to frontrun and we outline situations where HFT harms or benefits the large trader. Specifically, the front-running HFT is favorable to the large trader when (1) there is sufficient high-speed noise trading; (2) the high-speed noise trading is inadequate but her prediction is inaccurate enough.
texts.text: In this paper, through an extended celebrated Kyle's [2] model, we focus on the influences of an HFT on an informed large trader whose future order is detected by the former. We prove that it is optimal for HFT to frontrun and we outline situations where HFT harms or benefits the large trader. Specifically, the front-running HFT is favorable to the large trader when (1) there is sufficient high-speed noise trading; (2) the high-speed noise trading is inadequate but her prediction is inaccurate enough.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We also explore the influences of noise trading and prediction accuracy on investors' behavior and profits. We prove that HFT trades more aggressively with more high-speed noise trading and a more accurate prediction. For the large trader, her trading intensity and profit also increase with the size of high-speed noise trading. Surprisingly, we find that, when HFT's prediction is vague enough, the large trader can be guaranteed to be unharmed, but her profit tends to decrease as HFT's signal gets noisier, which seems counterintuitive with the common sense that the large trader gets better if her trading intentions are deeper hidden.
texts.text: We also explore the influences of noise trading and prediction accuracy on investors' behavior and profits. We prove that HFT trades more aggressively with more high-speed noise trading and a more accurate prediction. For the large trader, her trading intensity and profit also increase with the size of high-speed noise trading. Surprisingly, we find that, when HFT's prediction is vague enough, the large trader can be guaranteed to be unharmed, but her profit tends to decrease as HFT's signal gets noisier, which seems counterintuitive with the common sense that the large trader gets better if her trading intentions are deeper hidden.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The limit results of the above influences are also investigated. We have drawn two interesting conclusions: (1) when almost all noise traders are slow, HFT nearly does nothing, but the large trader is still harmed; (2) when the prediction is perfect and the size of high-speed noise trading tends to infinity, HFT reaches the max front-running volume, which is only half of the large trader's incoming order.
texts.text: The limit results of the above influences are also investigated. We have drawn two interesting conclusions: (1) when almost all noise traders are slow, HFT nearly does nothing, but the large trader is still harmed; (2) when the prediction is perfect and the size of high-speed noise trading tends to infinity, HFT reaches the max front-running volume, which is only half of the large trader's incoming order.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Besides the case where HFT predicts the large trader's order, we also study the case where HFT can predict the aggregate order flow, i.e., the large trader's order and its accompanying noise orders. From a practical point of view, this kind of HFTs has more advanced technology to estimate the trend of the whole market, rather than only the major market trend. In this case, we find that HFT still front-runs and always benefits the large trader.
texts.text: Besides the case where HFT predicts the large trader's order, we also study the case where HFT can predict the aggregate order flow, i.e., the large trader's order and its accompanying noise orders. From a practical point of view, this kind of HFTs has more advanced technology to estimate the trend of the whole market, rather than only the major market trend. In this case, we find that HFT still front-runs and always benefits the large trader.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.1. Related literature
texts.text: 1.1. Related literature

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Front-running strategy exploits other investors' need to trade, thus belonging to the topic of predatory trading. In the pioneering work Brunnermeier and Pedersen (2005) [3], only permanent price impact is considered and front-runners bring inferior prices to the large trader. Bessembinder et al. (2016) [4] considers transient price impact. The authors find that when the market is quite resilient, front-runners could benefit the large trader. A resilient market can be regarded as active and liquid, which is consistent with our discovery that HFT may be good for the large trader in a market with abundant noise trading. Other related works include Carlin et al. (2007) [5] and Sch¨ oneborn and Schied (2009) [6]. In the above works, predatory traders are perfectly aware of large trader's intention, while in this paper, the impacts of prediction accuracy are further investigated.
texts.text: Front-running strategy exploits other investors' need to trade, thus belonging to the topic of predatory trading. In the pioneering work Brunnermeier and Pedersen (2005) [3], only permanent price impact is considered and front-runners bring inferior prices to the large trader. Bessembinder et al. (2016) [4] considers transient price impact. The authors find that when the market is quite resilient, front-runners could benefit the large trader. A resilient market can be regarded as active and liquid, which is consistent with our discovery that HFT may be good for the large trader in a market with abundant noise trading. Other related works include Carlin et al. (2007) [5] and Sch¨ oneborn and Schied (2009) [6]. In the above works, predatory traders are perfectly aware of large trader's intention, while in this paper, the impacts of prediction accuracy are further investigated.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When it comes to high-frequency trading, Li (2018) [7] models front-running HFTs who predict the sum of informed and noise orders and concludes that informed trader trades less aggressively in the presence of HFTs. Yang and Zhu (2020) [8] models back-runners who use information about trading history to predict the informed traders' future path and trade along with them. The main difference between this paper and the above works is that different-size high-speed noise trading is considered, which is proved to be crucial for HFT's influences. For a more thorough review of high-frequency trading literature, readers could refer to Menkveld (2016) [9].
texts.text: When it comes to high-frequency trading, Li (2018) [7] models front-running HFTs who predict the sum of informed and noise orders and concludes that informed trader trades less aggressively in the presence of HFTs. Yang and Zhu (2020) [8] models back-runners who use information about trading history to predict the informed traders' future path and trade along with them. The main difference between this paper and the above works is that different-size high-speed noise trading is considered, which is proved to be crucial for HFT's influences. For a more thorough review of high-frequency trading literature, readers could refer to Menkveld (2016) [9].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Consequently, we contribute to the front-running literature by studying its influences on the large trader under various market conditions and different prediction accuracy, and some unexpected results are obtained.
texts.text: Consequently, we contribute to the front-running literature by studying its influences on the large trader under various market conditions and different prediction accuracy, and some unexpected results are obtained.

texts.text: 2. The Model and Equilibrium

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We start by introducing the following two-period model ( t = 0 , 1 , 2) for front-running, which is a variant of the classic Kyle's model.
texts.text: We start by introducing the following two-period model ( t = 0 , 1 , 2) for front-running, which is a variant of the classic Kyle's model.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this market, a risky asset is traded whose true value, v , is normally distributed as
texts.text: In this market, a risky asset is traded whose true value, v , is normally distributed as

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are four types of market participants: (1) dealers , who are assumed to be competitive and risk-neutral; (2) a normal-speed large informed trader (IT, for short), who privately knows v ; (3) a High-Frequency Trader (HFT, for short), who can get a signal about IT's future trading and might act as a front-runner; (4) noise traders , who trade randomly.
texts.text: There are four types of market participants: (1) dealers , who are assumed to be competitive and risk-neutral; (2) a normal-speed large informed trader (IT, for short), who privately knows v ; (3) a High-Frequency Trader (HFT, for short), who can get a signal about IT's future trading and might act as a front-runner; (4) noise traders , who trade randomly.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When ( θ 1 , θ z ) is in the first area, HFT cannot harm IT even if her signal is perfectly accurate since the market provides enough noise shelter for IT. When ( θ 1 , θ z ) is in the second area, IT is benefited when HFT's signal is relatively vague. What's more, larger the θ 1, smaller the θ z . That is, as the size of noise trading gets larger, less signal noise is needed to disturb HFT.

texts.orig: For IT, she also profits more as θ 1 increases, as shown in Figure 2. On the one hand, a larger θ 1 brings a larger β ∗ , which increases time-1 price impact, but this impact is also decreased by more noise trading. On the other hand, a larger β ∗ means that HFT provides more liquidity back at time 2. On average, IT trades more aggressively with the greater size of high-speed noise trading. When θ 1 is large enough ( θ 1 ≥ 0 . 15), the advantages of a large θ 1 outweigh the disadvantages, thus IT is benefited by HFT.

texts.orig: Next, we do comparative static analyses w.r.t. θ z : θ z changes in [0 , 25] , given θ 1 = 0 . 12 , 0 . 2 and 1 . For HFT, the trading intensity β ∗ decreases with θ z , as shown in (a)-(c) of Figure 3. Surprising results appear for HFT's expected profit. It is usually considered that HFT's profit should decrease if she receives a less accurate signal. However, as shown in (d) of Figure 3, it increases with θ z when both θ 1 and θ z are relatively small. It is because IT trades more as θ z gets larger ((a) in Figure 4), which brings more price impact for HFT to trade on.
texts.text: Next, we do comparative static analyses w.r.t. θ z : θ z changes in [0 , 25] , given θ 1 = 0 . 12 , 0 . 2 and 1 . For HFT, the trading intensity β ∗ decreases with θ z , as shown in (a)-(c) of Figure 3. Surprising results appear for HFT's expected profit. It is usually considered that HFT's profit should decrease if she receives a less accurate signal. However, as shown in (d) of Figure 3, it increases with θ z when both θ 1 and θ z are relatively small. It is because IT trades more as θ z gets larger ((a) in Figure 4), which brings more price impact for HFT to trade on.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 4 shows the trend of θ z 's impact on IT, corresponding to the results in Proposition 2. As the signal becomes noisier, HFT's action β ∗ decreases. On the one hand, HFT's time-1 trading causes less impact. On the other hand, her time-2 trading shares fewer transaction costs for IT. When the first effect exceeds the second one, IT's action and profit increase with θ z , otherwise, they decrease with it.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. When HFT Predicts the Aggregate Order
texts.text: 5. When HFT Predicts the Aggregate Order

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we discuss an extension of the original model that HFT can predict i + u 2 , i.e., the signal received by HFT is

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Li (2018) [7] models a market with normal-speed noise traders, i.e., θ 1 = 0, where front-running HFTs predict i + u 2 . The author concludes that IT is always harmed by HFT. The key points which make our conclusions different are: (1) [7] models stale dealers who quote linearly with unchanged impact coefficients, while we assume that dealers quote according to the weak-efficiency condition; (2) [7] assumes θ 1 = 0. Thus [7]'s results and our results can be seen as mutually complementary, explaining how front-running HFT affects IT when confronted with different liquidity providers and different sizes of market noise.
texts.text: Li (2018) [7] models a market with normal-speed noise traders, i.e., θ 1 = 0, where front-running HFTs predict i + u 2 . The author concludes that IT is always harmed by HFT. The key points which make our conclusions different are: (1) [7] models stale dealers who quote linearly with unchanged impact coefficients, while we assume that dealers quote according to the weak-efficiency condition; (2) [7] assumes θ 1 = 0. Thus [7]'s results and our results can be seen as mutually complementary, explaining how front-running HFT affects IT when confronted with different liquidity providers and different sizes of market noise.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6. Conclusion
texts.text: 6. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We study the influences of a front-running HFT on a large informed trader in various situations, where the size of high-speed noise trading and prediction accuracy differ. Since HFT takes liquidity away as well as provides liquidity back, she has a two-sided effect on the large trader. When the high-speed noise trading is sufficient, even if the large trader's intention is perfectly detected, she could be favored by HFT. Without enough noise shelter from the market, the large trader is benefited only when HFT's prediction is relatively vague.
texts.text: We study the influences of a front-running HFT on a large informed trader in various situations, where the size of high-speed noise trading and prediction accuracy differ. Since HFT takes liquidity away as well as provides liquidity back, she has a two-sided effect on the large trader. When the high-speed noise trading is sufficient, even if the large trader's intention is perfectly detected, she could be favored by HFT. Without enough noise shelter from the market, the large trader is benefited only when HFT's prediction is relatively vague.
